\noindent The 95\% upper bound on a $\chi^2$ distributed variable with (10 - 1) degrees of freedom is

\par\addvspace{\topsep}
\begingroup
\fvset{listparameters={%
  \setlength{\topsep}{0pt}%
  \setlength{\partopsep}{0pt}%
  \setlength{\parsep}{0pt}%
  \setlength{\itemsep}{0pt}%
}}
\begin{Verbatim}[breaklines=true,formatcom=\color{ada_blue}]
qchisq(p = .95, df = 9)
\end{Verbatim}
\begin{Verbatim}[breaklines=true,formatcom=\color{ada_light_blue}]
[1] 16.91898
\end{Verbatim}
\endgroup
\par\addvspace{\topsep}

\noindent Similarly, to calculate the upper bound for $\sigma^2$, we start with the lower bound of a $\chi^2$ distributed variable
